coredata.dc:title: Constructing hierarchical heterojunction structure for K/Co co-substituted Na3V2(PO4)3 by integrating carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Zeyi
Chen, Yanjun
Sun, Shiqi
Liu, Honglang
Wang, Chao
Huang, Que
Liu, Changcheng
Wang, Yanzhong
Guo, Li
coredata.dc:description: Na3V2(PO4)3 (NVP) has been widely adopted as cathode in sodium ion battery devices. Nevertheless, the weak intrinsic conductivity and serious structural collapse limit the further development. Herein, a simultaneous modified strategy of doping K/Co and integrating carbon quantum dots (CQD) is proposed. Substituting K+ is beneficial to afford amount of Na+ transport within the stabled and expanded lattice. The introduction of Co2+ generates beneficial hole carriers to improve conductivity. Furthermore, the bonding of conductive CQD guides to obtain nano-sized NVP grains, reducing the pathway for ionic migration to accelerate the diffusion capability. Importantly, a unique p-n type heterojunction construction is established in the interface between CQD (n-type) and NVP (p-type). This heterojunction structure enhances the mobility of electrons owing to the free pathways, in which the electrons transport in a relatively lower energy level without the scatter and collision of anions dopants. Ultimately, K0.1Na2.95V1.95Co0.05(PO4)3@CQD exhibits with the best energy output level. It’s initial capacity under 5C is 109.8 mA h g−1 and the retention is 87.6% after cycle 400 cycles. Even at 20 and 50C, its output is 93.5 and 82.6 mA h g−1 for 1st and 66.6 and 52.1 mA h g−1 for 1000th cycle, respectively. Finally, an asymmetric full cell test confirms its application practically.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Na3V2(PO4)3
CQD
Heterojunction structure
Sodium ion batteries
K/Co co-substituted

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Conversion and Storage and Environmental Technologies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) All the XRD patterns. (b) Refinement plot of KC0.05@CQD. (c) Structure representation for KC0.05@CQD. The particle size distribution and the SEM images of KC0.05@CQD (d, g), KC0.05 (e, h) and NVP (f, i). (j-l) TEM images of KC0.05@CQD.
(a) N2 adsorption - desorption isotherms of KC0.05@CQD. (b) The Raman profile of KC0.05@CQD. The XPS spectrum of C1s (c), V2p (d), K2p (e) and Co2p (f) in KC0.05@CQD.

(a) HRTEM image of KC0.05@CQD with schematic illustrate of heterojunction interface insert. (b) The band structure of the interfacial heterojunction. The UV results (c) and the XPS valence spectrums(d) of KC0.05 and CQD.

GCD curves (a), capacity at various rate (b), capacity at 1C (c) and retention contrast (d) of all cathodes. The capacity at 5C (e) and 10C (f) of KC0.05@CQD cathode. (g) Cycle capacity at superior rate of KC0.05@CQD. (h) The comprehensive performance contrasts.

(a) The CV curves of three samples. (b) CV curves at changed scan rate for KC0.05@CQD. (c) The capacity contribution analysis of KC0.05@CQD. (d) Nyquist curves of all samples. (e) The fitted Z’-ω-0.5 linear relationship. (f) ‘Potential vs. Time’ for KC0.05@CQD under GITT. (g) The collected ‘DNa+ vs. Potential’ curve for KC0.05@CQD.
Illustration of conduction mechanism for KC0.05@CQD materials.
(a) Illustration of Bi2Se3/KC0.05@CQD full cell. (b) GCD curve of the full cell. (c) Rate and cycle capacity of Bi2Se3/KC0.05@CQD full cell.
DNa+ of all samples based on euqation (3).
Collection of σ, Rct and DNa+ values for all samples.

head.ce:title: Constructing hierarchical heterojunction structure for K/Co co-substituted Na3V2(PO4)3 by integrating carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Energy Materials and Systems Institute, North University of China, Taiyuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Energy Materials and Systems Institute

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Energy Materials and Systems Institute, North University of China, Taiyuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment and Safety Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment and Safety Engineering, North University of China, Taiyuan, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Na3V2(PO4)3 (NVP) has been widely adopted as cathode in sodium ion battery devices. Nevertheless, the weak intrinsic conductivity and serious structural collapse limit the further development. Herein, a simultaneous modified strategy of doping K/Co and integrating carbon quantum dots (CQD) is proposed. Substituting K+ is beneficial to afford amount of Na+ transport within the stabled and expanded lattice. The introduction of Co2+ generates beneficial hole carriers to improve conductivity. Furthermore, the bonding of conductive CQD guides to obtain nano-sized NVP grains, reducing the pathway for ionic migration to accelerate the diffusion capability. Importantly, a unique p-n type heterojunction construction is established in the interface between CQD (n-type) and NVP (p-type). This heterojunction structure enhances the mobility of electrons owing to the free pathways, in which the electrons transport in a relatively lower energy level without the scatter and collision of anions dopants. Ultimately, K0.1Na2.95V1.95Co0.05(PO4)3@CQD exhibits with the best energy output level. It’s initial capacity under 5C is 109.8 mA h g−1 and the retention is 87.6% after cycle 400 cycles. Even at 20 and 50C, its output is 93.5 and 82.6 mA h g−1 for 1st and 66.6 and 52.1 mA h g−1 for 1000th cycle, respectively. Finally, an asymmetric full cell test confirms its application practically.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few decades, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have occupied the main market in the field of available energy device like mobile power, portable energy, etc. [1–4]. However, the soaring cost caused by the deficiency in lithium resources has greatly restricted its research progress and applications [5–7]. While, sodium ion batteries (SIBs) have emerged to be perfect candidate due to the abundant sodium reserves and the similar chemical property, possessing great development in energy storage fields especially the large-scale equipment. [8,9]. Thus, SIBs have gained amount of surveys and researches currently. However, the migration of Na+ with larger radius in the electrode results in the serious volume collapse, which requires suit electrode materials with stable channels and construction for the successfully reversible de/intercalation of Na+. For SIBs, most of the current anode materials possess higher capacity than the cathode materials, so the property of cathode material basically determines the sodium storage performance of SIBs. Currently, there are various materials like metal oxide type [10–14], Prussian blue - type structure [15–17], phosphates - type materials [18–22] etc., have been developed to be cathodes for SIBs. Among them, phosphates materials have attracted great attention owing to its stable and open framework.

Na3V2(PO4)3, denoted as NVP, a classical NASICON polyphosphate - type material, have been a befitting candidate with great property [23–26]. The 3D V2(PO4)3 framework possesses beneficial channels within the stable structure to afford the reversible de/intercalation of Na+. Significantly, NVP can express a stable potential platform at 3.4 V, output a high capacity (117.6 mA h g−1) theoretically. However, its low intrinsic electronic and ionic conduction have limited the rate property. Especially, the serious volume collapse during Na+ shuttle process seriously impacts structure of NVP, leading to huge decrease of capacity. On account of these challenges, many investigations concerning about modifying the NVP to improve electrochemical performance have been greatly developed, and the major effective methods include bulk phase doping [27–29], nano-sized particles [30–33] and carbon materials composite [34–36]. Specifically, it has been reported [37–39] that introducing pillar ion K+ with larger ion radius could efficiently afford the reversible shuttle of Na+ within the expanded framework of NVP. In addition, substituting the V3+ site with variant-valence Co2+ will enhance the electrons conductivity because of the generated hole carriers [40–42]. Furthermore, the carbon quality dots (CQD) possess admirable conductivity [43], efficiently enhancing the electronic conductivity of electrode materials. Interestingly, the CQD, as a semi-conductor, together with mixed ion–electron conductor such as NVP could ingeniously establish the heterojunction structure in the interface between the two phases. The interfacial heterojunction could greatly increase electronic mobility so that the property of electrode could be improved effectively.

In this work, we improve the property of NVP through combine the strategies of bulk doping (K/Co) and carbon integrating (CQD). In terms of crystal structure, K and Co occupy the Na1 and V site, respectively, bringing a synergy effect to expand the framework and generate hole carriers, improving the Na+ and electrons shuttle process. Moreover, compositing CQD could establish a unique heterojunction construction in the surface between KC0.05 sample and CQD. The UV and XPS characterizations demonstrate that the KC0.05 possess relatively more hole carriers generated by different-valence Co2+ ions substituting the V3+ site, while the major carriers in CQD are electrons. Thus, this p-n type interfacial heterojunction is generated based on the p-KC0.05 and n-CQD conductors. Accordingly, the heterojunction could greatly enhance the mobility of electrons owing to its free pathway for electrons, because the electrons could transport in a relatively lower energy level without the scatter and collision of anions dopants. Thus, this perfect configuration of K0.1Na2.95V1.95Co0.05(PO4)3@CQD composite makes it an excellent cathode with outstanding performance. Specifically, the capacity at 5C of KC0.05@CQD is 109.8 and 96.2 mA h g−1 for 1st and 400th, and the retention is high of 87.6%. Even at ultrahigh current density, its capacity still is 93.5 and 82.6 mA h g−1 for 1st under 20C and 50C, respectively, after 1000 laps, the capacity is 66.6 and 52.1 mA h g−1.

In Fig. 1 , K0.1Na2.95V1.95Co0.05(PO4)3@CQD, K0.1Na2.95V1.95Co0.05(PO4)3 and Na3V2(PO4)3 samples were successfully prepared through sol–gel method, which are labeled as KC0.05@CQD, KC0.05 and NVP, respectively. The detail descripts about preparation procedure, battery assemble, corresponding characterization and electrochemical tests are in the supporting information.

Fig. 2 a containing XRD patterns to detect the crystal structure of all samples. It can be observed that all diffraction patterns of three samples point to the R-3c space group related with PDF card (00–053-0018) without any impurity peaks, suggesting that the pure NASICON construction exists in the materials and the unique NVP framework still maintains even after doping K and Co into bulk cell. In the enlarger part, it can be seen clearly that KC0.05@CQD possesses an extra peak at approximately 26.5 ° , which belongs to the (002) plane of CQD, strongly proving the combination with CQD in this KC0.05@CQD material. What’s more, the (006) peak center of KC0.05@CQD exhibits a slight offset to the lower degree, implying the expanded distance of crystal plane based on the Bragg formula. Accordingly, a larger volume of crystal cell can be obtained for K/Co co-doped samples, which can be explained by the pillar effect arise from larger K+ supporting the framework. To quantify the degree of cell expansion, the cell parameters of all samples have been calculated by Highscore software, which are presented in Table 1 , Fig. 2b and Fig. S1a, b with a good credibility due to the low values of Rp, Rwp and Rexp. Furthermore, the specific atomic occupations of K and Co dopants in KC0.05@CQD are determined by Rietveld refinement through GSAS software, which are displayed in Table S1 and Fig. S1c with great rationality according to awesome fit degree. Obviously, the occupations are 0.0910 and 0.0250 for K in Na1 and Co in V, respectively, agreeing well with the experimental data. Thus, we can distinctly outline the crystal construction of KC0.05@CQD as illustrated in Fig. 2c. The K+ replace the Na1, supporting the NASICON framework along with the z-axis due to its pillar effect to favorably improve the reversibility and accelerate the migration of Na+ in Na2 sites without loss of capacity. While the smaller Co2+ partly replaces the V3+ site to generate the [CoO6] group, producing some profitable hole carriers, further enhancing the intrinsic electronic conductivity and increasing the interspace of cell to promote the Na+ transport.

What’s more, comparing the XRD profiles of three samples (Fig. 1a), it’s worth to be note that KC0.05@CQD sample exhibits a relatively lower peak intensity, indicating its lower crystallinity, which may be resulted from the smaller-sized particles due to the limited crystal growth derived from the combination with CQD. This can be further confirmed by contrasting and analyzing the SEM images. Fig. 2d, e and f are the particle size distributions calculated by Nano-Measurement software based on the SEM images of KC0.05@CQD (g), KC0.05 (h) and NVP (i) under the same magnification, respectively. It is obvious that KC0.05@CQD material possesses smaller particles with more uniform distribution, which can be clearly observed in SEM images under larger magnification (Fig. S2(a-i)), and its average particle size is 107 nm, far smaller than those of KC0.05 (477.05 nm) and NVP (750 nm). As for the morphological features, KC0.05@CQD materials are consist of nano-sized irregular particles with 5 nm-thick amorphous carbon coating surface. Besides, the homogeneously distributed elements (C, Na, V, P, O, K, Co) over the whole particle are demonstrated by EDS images shown in Fig. S2j. To further observe the microstructure of KC0.05@CQD, TEM images are captured in Fig. S4 and Fig. 2j-l. In Fig. 2j, distinctly, there are many irregular grains with small size and we can clearly observe the existence of CQD and the material is dispersed uniformly on the carbon coated NVP grains. Furthermore, the CQD array are existed in the NVP lattice fringe more obviously shown in Fig. 2k, and the FFT image demonstrates the great crystalline of KC0.05. Obviously, Fig. 2l shows that three types of lattice fringes with different space value are existed in the composite. The plane distance of 0.314 nm belongs to the (002) plane of CQD. The space values of 0.258 and 0.376 nm are consistent with the (018) and (113) planes in KC0.05, respectively. Generally, the HRTEM images demonstrate the successful introduction of conductive CQD material into the NVP system as designed. This unique morphology and construction could stimulate the great electronic transport property due to the strong connection between carbon layers and CQD.

Moreover, the surface area and porosity of three samples are characterized by N2 adsorption–desorption test and the result profiles are shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. S5a, b. It can be found that all isotherms show the characterization corresponding to IV- type, meaning that there are meso- and micro- pores formed by the appropriate stacking of nano-sized particles. Specifically, the KC0.05@CQD composite possesses highest area of 16.98 m2 g−1 and most uniform pore size of 4 nm, which are higher than that of NVP (10.93 m2 g−1) and KC0.05 (13.8 m2 g−1). For KC0.05@CQD, the large area could increase the connect sites to promote the Na+ transport in the interface. Meanwhile, the large pore volume can work as buffer for the electrode volume expand during charge/discharge process. This unique micro- architecture of KC0.05@CQD makes it an excellent cathode with awesome stability during the long-term cycle at high current density. Fig. 3b displays the Raman spectrum of KC0.05@CQD sample, in which d-band and G-band characterize peaks locate in 1358 cm -1 and 1596 cm−1, respectively [44], with the ID/IG ratio is 1.15, suggesting a great graphitization of carbon materials in KC0.05@CQD composite.

As for the chemical nature of KC0.05@CQD material, XPS measurement is adopted to reveal the chemical state of elements. As drawn in Fig. S5c, K and Co dopants are successfully detected in the survey spectrum, indicating the occupation of K and Co in the NVP. Specifically, Fig. 3c is C1s spectrum, in which three characterized peaks occur. The CC peak emerges in 284.4 eV, indicating free electrons are existed within π-bond to improve electronic conductivity, and the peak in 285.7 eV and 288.4 eV point to the CO and CO band, respectively. In Fig. 3d, there are two peaks in the V2p spectrum locating at 516.43 eV and 523.53 eV, pointing to the V2p3/2 and V2p1/2 [45], demonstrating that V3+ valence is not affected by K and Co dopants. Besides, in K2p spectrum of Fig. 3e, the peaks of 292.93 eV and 295.88 eV arise from K+2p3/2 and K+2p1/2, respectively. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 3f, there are two pairs of peaks in Co2p, the peaks of 781.38 eV and 786.43 eV belong to the Co2p3/2 and its satellite, with energy difference of 5.505 eV [46]. The Co2p1/2 locates in 797.63 eV, and its satellite peak occurs in 803.33 eV with the energy above 5.7 eV, confirming the + 2 oxidation state of Co ion [47].

The optimized KC0.05@CQD composite is composed of carbon coated KC0.05 particles and conductive CQD. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the HRTEM of KC0.05@CQD sample has clearly displayed this morphology. This configuration is facilitated to improve the electronic transport of electrode. What’s more, the interface between KC0.05 particle and CQD provides a space to establish an unique heterojunction construction and the corresponding schematic is illustrated in the insert chart. As we can know, the KC0.05 sample is a kind of modified NVP system, which is essentially a mixed ion-electronic conductor, possessing conductivity of ions and electrons. It is worth to note that the NVP contains both conduction band and valence band, which are denoted as Ec and Ev, respectively. In addition, the strong-conductive CQD are semi-conductors with significant absorption in the ultraviolet range. Hence, the interface between KC0.05 and CQD can naturally form a heterojunction construction. To further detect the band structure of the KC0.05@CQD sample, the UV measurement and XPS valence spectrum are adopted to characterize the band gap and the location of valence band relative to Fermi energy level (EF). The bad gap (Eg) represents the energy different between Ev and Ec, meaning the required energy for an electron jumping from Ev to Ec, which can be detected by UV measurement. As shown in Fig. S6, the UV absorption profiles of KC0.05 and CQD are recorded in the range of 200–800 nm, the absorption peaks are at about 420 nm for KC0.05 and 300 nm for CQD. The Eg can be roughly estimated based on the empirical formula (equation (1)). To precisely calculate the value of Eg, we reference the Tauc plot [48] (equation (2)) and the results have been drawn in Fig. 4c.
(1)
E g
=
1240 λ
(2)
( α h v )
1 / n
= A
( h v -
E g
)

In equation (2), the αhv, hv and Eg represent the absorption, light quantum energy and band gap, respectively. The profile of ‘(αhv)1/
n vs hv’ can be utilized to calculate the value of Eg by extension method, the extense straight part of profile and plot ‘y = 0′ intersect in a point, which represents the Eg.

Specifically, the Tauc plots of KC0.05 and CQD samples are displayed in Fig. 4c. It is clear that the intercept representing the Eg is sited at 3.44 eV and 4.04 eV for KC0.05 and CQD, respectively. Furthermore, the XPS valence spectrum can confirm the position of Ev by calculating the value of (EF-Ev), which is also calculated by extension method. The corresponding results are displayed in Fig. 4d. It can be found that the values of (EF-Ev) are 0.82 eV for KC0.05 and 2.07 eV for CQD. Thus, the band structure of KC0.05 and CQD can be deduced based on the Eg and (EF-Ev), comprehensively. The band structure of KC0.05@CQD composite is illustrated in Fig. 4b, which contains the interfacial heterojunction. Obviously, the Eg value of KC0.05 is 3.44 eV, and the corresponding EF is leveled in 0.82 eV above Ev, which is under the midline of band gap. According to the Fermi-Dirac distribution function, it can be known that the majority carriers of KC0.05 are holes. This is reasonable that the Co2+ ions behaving different valence state can generate more hole carriers as result of occupation in the V3+ site. All of this proves the p-type conductor of KC0.05. Similarity, the relatively more carriers in CQD are electrons based on the Eg of 4.04 eV and (EF-Ev) of 2.07 eV, indicating the CQD are n-type conductors. Naturally, the KC0.05@CQD composite contains a p-n type interfacial heterojunction. The EF of KC0.05 and CQD are in the same energy level and the energy band has been bended at the interface between two phases. Significantly, this heterojunction can further improve the mobility of electrons because the hole carriers can fall to the relatively lower energy level between the interfaces so that the hole can freely transport without the scatter and collision from Co2+ anions. Therefore, it is expected that the KC0.05@CQD material could deliver a greatly enhanced electrochemical performance especially in terms of rate capacity due to its high mobility of electrons.

Fig. 5 shows the electrochemical properties including rate and cycle performance. Specifically, all the initial GCD curves are displayed in Fig. 5a. Clearly, there is a platform at 3.4 V occurred in each sample, which is delivered from V3+/V4+ redox pair. The KC0.05@CQD sample possesses the longest platform, demonstrating its highest discharge capacity. In addition, the potential difference between charge and discharge platform represents the degree of polarization. The KC0.05@CQD reveals the lowest polarization, indicating the best reversibility arising from its high stable structure. Furthermore, the rate capacity of all samples is illustrated in Fig. 5b. The KC0.05@CQD sample can deliver capacities of 115.7, 113.9, 110.8, 109.5, 107.6, 101.8, 98.2, 95.6 and 107.2 mA h g−1 along with the changed rate. The rate capacity and corresponding maintain degree for KC0.05@CQD both obviously beyond KC0.05 and NVP. In addition, Fig. 5c exhibits the capacity under 1C of all electrodes. Distinctly, KC0.05@CQD delivers the highest capacity and columbic efficiency maintains at 100% during the prolonged process. The capacity for 1st under 1C is 114.2, 107.6 and 83.7 mA h g−1 for KC0.05@CQD, KC.05 and NVP, respectively, and the capacity for 400th can keep at the value of 100.1, 75.2 and 46.9 mA h g−1. Clearly, Fig. 5d contrasts the retention under 1C of KC0.05@CQD (87.6%), KC.05 (69.8%) and NVP (56.1%). To further detect the cycling performance of KC0.05@CQD, the cycle capacity under 5C (e) and 10C (Fig. 5f) are tested. Obviously, it delivers capacity of 109.8 and 96.2 mA h g−1 for 1st and 400th at 5C, suggesting the retention is high of 87.6%. At 10C current density, the capacity output of KC0.05@CQD still is 98.9 and 81 mA h g−1 for 1st and 500th, meaning the loss of capacity reach 0.036% per lap. Besides, its columbic efficiency at 5C and 10C is 100% almost in each cycle, which demonstrates the outstanding reversibility of KC0.05@CQD sample. Fig. 5g illustrates the long-term cycle property under superior current density. Specifically, the capacity under 20C of KC0.05@CQD is of 93.5 and 66.6 mA h g−1 for 1st and 1000th lap. Under 50C rate, its capacity is high of 82.6 and 52.1 mA h g−1 for the 1st and 1000th lap. These outstanding performance of KC0.05@CQD is contributed by its stable framework and interfacial heterojunction with provide accelerated Na+ migration and effective electronic mobility. Furthermore, we contrast the comprehensive performance of KC0.05@CQD sample with some previous cathodes in terms of capacity and retention. As shown in Fig. 5h, obviously, the KC0.05@CQD exhibits a relatively greater electrochemical performance than others.

Significantly, CV and EIS tests are conducted within 2.3–4.1 V at electrochemical workstation to further reveal the kinetic mechanism of cathodes. Specifically, Fig. 6 a contrasts all CV plots tested under 0.1 mV s−1. It is obvious that each electrode exhibit two peaks at reduce and oxide process, respectively, arise from the V3+/V4+ redox pair, occur in approximately 3.4 V. The peak intensity directly inflects the plot’s aera, which equal to the energy density, reaches the highest value in KC0.05@CQD cathode. Besides, the different of potential between re- and dox- peaks reveals the polarization, and KC0.05@CQD sample behaves lowest degree of polarization, indicating the best reversibility. Fig. 6b displays six CV profiles under different scan rate for KC0.05@CQD cathode, Fig. S7b for KC0.05 and Fig. S7c for NVP. Under this mode, the peak current (Ip ) is increasing with the enlargement of scan rate (v). Significantly, there is a linear function achieved between ‘Ip vs. v0.5 ′, which has been fitted in Fig. S7a for each cathode. Correspondingly, we can obtain the Na+ diffusion coefficients (DNa+ ) through this linear fitting base on equation (3) (Randles-Sevcik [49]) shown in following:
(3)
I p
=
2.69 ×
10 5
n
3 / 2
A
C
D
1 / 2
v
1 / 2

Table 2 summarizes the values of ‘Ip vs v 0.5′ slope and DNa+ in each cathode. Obviously, KC0.05@CQD sample possesses a comfortable DNa+ of 1.59 × 10-10 for charge process and 0.64 × 10-10 cm2 s−1 for discharge processes, 10 times higher than NVP. In addition, Ip and v follow the function of Ip = avb , and b reflects the control step duting Na+ shuttle process. Specifically, the b is 0.5 means the Na+ transport is controled by diffusion process, while b equals to 1, the control step is pseudocapacitive reaction. Therefore, the capacitive contribution can be calculated, as shown in equation (4):
(4)
i =
k 1
v +
k 2
v
0.5

To obtain the b value, the linear plots of ‘log(Ip ) vs. Log(v)’ for each cathode are fitted in Fig. S7d. Correspondingly, as shown in Fig. 6c, the capacitive contribution of KC0.05@CQD is 81.9%, which is much larger than that of KC0.05 (Fig. S7e, 65.46%) and NVP (Fig. S7f, 54.52%), demonstrating the excellent rate property of KC0.05@CQD. The CV results verifies the outstanding Na storage mechanism derived from the optimized crystal structure and the interfacial heterojunction construction of nano-sized KC0.05@CQD particles.

Fig. 6d shows the Nyquist plots for each cathode obtained from EIS test conducted in SOC of 3.4 V. Obviously, combining with equation (5), each Nyquist curve possesses the similar shape which can be divided to a semicycle and a incline line, specifically, the intercept of semocycle section is equal to the charge transfer resistance (Rct ). Furthermore, the incline line section represents to the Warburg impedance (Zw ), in which the Z’ and ω-0.5 follow a linear relationship, which has been fitted in Fig. 6e for each cathode, and we can directly obtain DNa+ value through the corresponding slope, as exhibited in equation (6):
(5)
Z
re
=
R s
+
R
ct
+
σ ω
- 1 / 2
(6)
D
N
a +
=
R 2
T 2
2
A 2
n 4
F 4
C
Na
2
σ 2

Table 3 shows the values of Rct , Zw and DNa+ in each cathode. It can be deduced that KC0.05@CQD behaviors with the best kinetic with the lowest Rct (127.9 Ω) and highest DNa+ (0.68 × 10-12 cm2 s−1), which means KC0.05@CQD possesses the outstanding Na+ storage and electrons transport mechanism.

What’s more, we also detect DNa+ of KC0.05@CQD in the whole charge/discharge process through GITT mode. Fig. 6f displays the curve of ‘Potential vs. Time’ in the whole GITT process. Specifically, in the charge section, the cathode works in an alternating current, whose value changes from 0 to 0.1C then turn to 0 and so on, the corresponding state of cathode changes from steady to charge then turn to steady and so forth. And cathode possesses the similar state change during discharge process. In a period, as shown in Fig. S8a and Fig. S8c, there are three important indexes of τ,
Δ
E τ
and
Δ
E s
, which represens the charge/discharge time, the potential change during charge/discharge time and the potential difference between two steady ststes. Furthermore, we could calculate DNa+ in every period according to equation (7). Can equation (7) be simplified to equation (8) only the linear function achieved in relationship of ‘Potential vs τ1/2′, which have been proved in Fig. S8b and Fig. S8d for charge and discharge period. Therefore, the ‘DNa+ vs. Potential’ are collected in Fig. 6g and the corresponding values are beyond 10-9 cm2 s−1, representing the great kinetics of KC0.05@CQD sample. What’s more, there is a clear low ebb in Fig. 6g at the 3.4 V point, in which the phase transfer reaction occurs from Na3V2(PO4)3 to Na1V2(PO4)3.
(7)
D
N
a +
=
4 π
m
V m
MA
2
Δ
E s
τ

(8)

N

MA
2
Δ
E s
Δ
E τ
2

Comprehensively, Fig. 7 illustrates conduction mechanism for KC0.05@CQD. The nano-sized particles are composed by the modified carbon-coated KC0.05 with conductive CQD dispersing on the surface. This configuration can not only shorten the pathways for Na+ transport due to its small size of 100 nm, but also enhance the electronic conductivity due to the improved connection property between the conducted regent CQD and carbon layer. In one hand, the optimized KC0.05 crystal possesses a stable and expended framework to afford Na+ migration owing to K+ and Co2+ occupation, which bring synergy effect to improve reversibility during the charge/discharge process. In the other hand, the efficiently conductive CQD, acting as semi-conductors, disperse on the surface of the KC0.05 mixed ion–electron conductor to supply the interface between two phases to generate the beneficial p-n type heterojunction construction, which can further improve the mobility of electrons. Benefiting from the unique heterojunction structure, the carriers can fall to the relatively lower energy level so that the carriers can freely pass through without the scatter and collision from anions. Therefore, the fast transport pathways for both Na+ and electrons have been successfully established in KC0.05@CQD composite, leading to outstanding electrochemical performance and kinetic characteristics.

The application of KC0.05@CQD as cathodes has been further tested in Bi2Se3//KC0.05@CQD full cell device, as drawn in Fig. 8 a. Based on the potential (vs. (Na+/Na)) of KC0.05@CQD and Bi2Se3 and their GCD curves (Fig. S9 for Bi2Se3), the electrochemical tests for this full cell were recorded within 0–3.6 V. Specifically, GCD plot is shown in Fig. 8b, in which there are three and two plateaus in the charge and discharge process, respectively, resulted by Bi2Se3 (0.4, 0.6, 1.2 V) and KC0.05@CQD (3.4 V). And the corresponding discharge capacity and voltage is 117.2 mA h g−1 and 2.3 V, means the energy density reaches a high value of 271.6 W h Kg−1. In addition, Fig. 8c shows the rate and cycle property of KC0.05@CQD, its capacity at various rate is 116.4, 98.2, 90.8, 82.6 and 72.4 mA h g−1, and the subsequent cycle capacity under 1C is 74.9 mA h g−1 for 1st and 64.3 mA h g−1 for 100th lap, demonstrates the retention reaches 85.8%. In summary, this full cell exhibits an excellent electrochemical performance, indicating the KC0.05@CQD material is a suit cathode for SIBs.

In summary, the property of NVP could be effectively improved by co-substituting K/Co and compositing CQD. The optimized KC0.05@CQD samples are consisted of nano-sized KC0.05 particles with CQD dispersed on the surface. Specifically, expanded Na+ transport channels are generated inner the KC0.05crystal, accelerating the ionic conductivity efficiently. Moreover, the conductive regent CQD possess more electron carriers and a heterojunction construction can be formed in the interface between two phases, which has been confirmed through UV measurement and XPS valence spectrum. Comprehensively, KC0.05@CQD sample displays an outstanding electrochemical performance. In detail, its capacity output under 5C is 109.8 mA h g−1 for 1st and 96.2 mA h g−1 for 400th lap, the corresponding retention is 87.6%. Even at ultrahigh current densities, the output at 20C still reaches 93.5 mA h g−1 for 1st and 66.6 mA h g−1 for 1000th. While under 50C rate, the capacity is 82.6 mA h g−1 for 1st and 52.1 mA h g−1 for 1000th lap. What’s more, kinetic behaviors testes imply the outstanding Na+ storage in KC0.05@CQD cathode. And the asymmetric full cell test confirms its application practically. Therefore, KC0.05@CQD can be indeed applied in SIBs.

Zeyi Tian: Methodology, Software, Writing – original draft. Yanjun Chen: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization, Investigation. Shiqi Sun: Supervision. Honglang Liu: Methodology, Software. Chao Wang: Supervision. Que Huang: Supervision, Resources, Formal analysis. Changcheng Liu: Supervision. Yanzhong Wang: Supervision, Resources, Software, Investigation. Li Guo: Visualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The present work is financially supported by Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (No. 20210302123044, No. 20210302123017, No. 20210302123067); Program for the Innovative Talents of Higher Education Institutions of Shanxi (PTIT). The authors also thanks Hengcong Qin from Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the XRD and XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2021.12.195.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
